completeness.rationale: The triage captured: CSV import as trigger, special characters as mechanism, ~200 task list size, encoding-related error message, and that saving had previously worked. Missing: OS and app version (listed as an information gap but never asked), and the manual-save vs auto-save distinction. Four of six expected items are present; two important ones are absent.

accuracy.rationale: The triage precisely matches the first acceptable diagnostic path: CSV import identified as trigger, encoding as mechanism, non-ASCII typographic characters as the specific vector. The root cause hypothesis correctly points to the save/serialization path mishandling non-ASCII characters and causing a crash instead of graceful error handling. The 64KB threshold is not discovered, but that is explicitly permitted by the acceptable paths.

thoroughness.rationale: The agent asked three well-chosen rounds of questions and reached a solid diagnosis. However, it never asked for OS or app version (standard triage basics, and it self-noted the gap). It also never asked whether auto-save triggers the same crash, which would have surfaced the manual-save-only nature of the bug. And it never suggested the reporter try removing or isolating just the imported tasks to confirm the CSV theory — a step that would have been highly informative and was within reach.

economy.rationale: Three turns, each clearly targeted at a different diagnostic dimension: crash characterization, temporal onset and size-dependency, and character/source specifics. No redundancy, no filler questions.

question_quality.rationale: The suggestion in turn 2 to test saving a small fresh task list was genuinely insightful — it confirmed the bug was data-specific rather than a general save failure without requiring the reporter to do complex isolation. Turn 3 probing the CSV source and character types was well-targeted. Turn 1 was solid but fairly generic crash characterization. Overall the questions showed real diagnostic reasoning.

actionability.rationale: The final summary is highly actionable: it includes a specific root cause hypothesis with Unicode code points called out, concrete reproduction steps, four discrete recommended fix steps (UTF-8 audit of save path, CSV import sanitization, graceful error handling, normalization option), and a proposed test case with specific character examples. A developer could begin investigation immediately.

notable_strengths: Correctly identified the acceptable diagnostic path (CSV import + encoding) within three turns despite a vague initial report
The small-list diagnostic test suggestion in turn 2 was a clever, low-effort isolation step that confirmed the data-specific nature of the bug
Final summary is exceptionally detailed and actionable — reproduction steps, fix recommendations, and a concrete test case with specific Unicode characters
notable_weaknesses: Never asked for OS version or app version — standard triage information that the agent itself flagged as a gap but failed to collect
Did not ask whether auto-save also crashes, missing the manual-save-only distinction
Did not ask the reporter to try removing or isolating just the imported tasks to directly confirm the CSV import theory before closing
most_insightful_question: Try creating a brand-new task list with just a few tasks and saving that — this efficiently confirmed the bug was tied to existing data rather than a general save failure, without requiring complex isolation from the reporter.
missed_opportunities: Ask for OS version and TaskFlow version — standard triage basics that were never collected
Ask whether auto-save (if it exists) also triggers a crash — would have surfaced the manual-save-only behavior

Ask the reporter to try deleting or moving the imported tasks out of the list to see if the crash disappears — would have strongly confirmed the CSV import as the direct cause rather than leaving it as a theory
